questions.question: The MCA is backed by a statutory Code of Practice. What does a care worker's legal duty to 'have regard to' the Code mean in practice?
options: You must follow it unless you have a good reason not to
It applies only to doctors and managers
You may ignore it because it is only guidance
You must follow it only when a manager is present

questions.question: For consent to a piece of everyday care to be valid, it must be:
options: Informed, given voluntarily, and given by someone with capacity for that decision
Renewed in writing every week
Given by a family member on the person's behalf
Written down and signed once, then filed away

questions.question: 'Informed' consent means the person understands what you are proposing, why you are proposing it, and:
options: how long you have worked in care
the names of everyone on shift
what the alternatives are, including the option of doing nothing at all
the cost of the care package

questions.question: Which type of Lasting Power of Attorney can be used while the donor still has capacity, if they agree?
options: Health and welfare LPA
Property and financial affairs LPA
An advance decision to refuse treatment
A Court of Protection deputyship

questions.question: An LPA is only legally valid once it has been registered with which body?
options: The Care Quality Commission
The local authority
The Office of the Public Guardian
The Court of Protection

questions.question: Which statement about an advance decision to refuse treatment (ADRT) is correct?
options: It can demand that a specific treatment is given
It can be made by a child of any age
It is binding only if a family member agrees
It can only refuse treatment, not demand a particular treatment

questions.question: Under the MCA, which of these best describes what counts as 'restraint'?
options: Only actions the person actively resists
Only help given against a doctor's advice
Only physical holds count as restraint
Physical holds, and also things like locking a door, using bed rails or covert medication

questions.question: A deprivation of liberty in a setting such as supported living or a person's own home is authorised by:
options: The Court of Protection
The managing authority
The Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards
The Office of the Public Guardian

questions.question: Which of the following is one of the six qualifying requirements assessed before a DoLS authorisation can be granted?
options: The person's length of residence
The consent of the next of kin
The person's financial means
No refusals (no valid conflicting advance decision or attorney/deputy decision)

questions.question: In the DoLS process, who is the 'managing authority'?
options: The care home or hospital providing the care
The Court of Protection
An Independent Mental Capacity Advocate
The local council

questions.question: The Liberty Protection Safeguards (LPS) were created by which piece of legislation?
options: The Mental Capacity (Amendment) Act 2019
The Mental Capacity Act 2005
The Care Act 2014
The Mental Health Act 1983

questions.question: A DoLS authorisation is time-limited to a maximum of:
options: 1 month
12 months
No maximum applies
6 months

questions.question: Under the 'acid test', does a person's compliance or lack of objection to their placement stop it being a deprivation of liberty?
options: Yes, if the person is content it is not a deprivation
Yes, if the placement is a good one
No — compliance or lack of objection does not stop it being a deprivation of liberty
Only if the person is actively trying to leave

questions.question: An unauthorised deprivation of liberty breaches a person's human rights under:
options: The Health and Safety at Work Act
The Data Protection Act 2018
The Equality Act 2010
Article 5 of the European Convention on Human Rights

questions.question: Under the MCA, a person may have capacity to choose what to wear yet lack capacity to make a complex decision about their finances.
options: True
False

questions.question: A single unwise choice is, on its own, enough to prove that a person lacks capacity.
options: True
False

questions.question: Restraint may be used for staff convenience or because a service is short-staffed.
options: True
False

questions.question: The Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards (DoLS) provide the legal process for authorising a deprivation of liberty for people in care homes and hospitals.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are practical ways to support a person to make their own decision? (Select all that apply)
options: Choose the time of day when the person is at their best
Find a quiet, familiar place free of distractions
Rush the person so the decision is made quickly
Use plain language, pictures, objects or a communication aid
Simply decide for them to save time

questions.question: When making a best-interests decision, the decision-maker must: (Select all that apply)
options: Consider whether the person is likely to regain capacity, and whether the decision can wait
Ignore the views of family and carers entirely
Decide purely on the basis of the person's age or appearance
Encourage and support the person to take part as far as they are able
Consider the person's past and present wishes and feelings

questions.question: For an advance decision to refuse life-sustaining treatment to be valid, it must: (Select all that apply)
options: Demand a specific alternative treatment
Be in writing
Be approved by a judge before it takes effect
Clearly state that it applies even if life is at risk
Be signed and witnessed

questions.question: Which two roles sit at the heart of the DoLS process? (Select all that apply)
options: The Office of the Public Guardian
The Care Quality Commission
The supervisory body (the local authority)
The managing authority (the care home or hospital)

questions.question: The MCA two-stage test begins with the diagnostic question: is there an impairment of, or disturbance in the functioning of, the person's mind or ___?
Restraint of a person who lacks capacity is only lawful when it is necessary and a ___ response to the likelihood and seriousness of the harm.
If a DoLS authorisation is granted, the person must have a Relevant Person's ___ to support them and to help them challenge the authorisation.
